author: @lasentinal
text:
BCE & CE are too close phonetically. 
I use BC to mean Backwards Chronology and AD to mean Advancing Dates, which is what they logically are.

Otherwise a very good lesson.

author: @sharonjuniorchess
text: The oldest counting system did not needs words or symbols for quantity it just used halving or doubling to arrive at the quantity. The aborigines as well as the Egyptians both used systems that were based on these binary properties. So you start with a pile of pebbles and reduce it down to a series of marks and you can build it back up again to the same quantity of pebbles.

author: @rfiskillingussoftly6568

For instance the months of the year, 28-29 days,30-31 days for 12 months  to equal 365"   when there is a natural 28 day cycle over 13 months to equal 364 days...add the one 0 day per year equals 365....if every month had 28 days every 1st day would be say Monday...and the 1st of every month would be a Monday!  Way less confusing!  # the human calculator!

author: @emel60
text:
Hi Robert!
I've been binge watching your energy related videos from this year and past winter. Exceptionally good content! You really contribute to the community, but there might be an even greater impact you could do!
Would you be willing to make some content relating myths and heresays, in the energy related topics? For instance, is it cheaper and more efficient to boil a... Liter of water in a kettle one cup, versus one liter at a time... Is it more efficient to heat up a boiler when its empty versus topping it up? Is it cheaper to boil a pot of water on a gas stove, or is it cheaper if you boil it first in the kettle and continue cooking afterwards.
You see my point. I feel these debunking/educational videos would actually have a positive impact!
Thanks!

replies.author: @databang
replies.text: (☝ ՞ਊ ՞) I reckon we just have some traditions that just seem quaint and more relatable to everyday human scaling. Would you like a teaspoon of sugar or 4.2 grams? Also it’s not like we’re against metric, we actually learn it, it’s just that we’re not anti-imperial despite 15/16ths of the World pretending as if they’ve moved onto metric utopia.

replies.author: @johnx9318
replies.text: @databang imperial used to be our tradition to, but we grew up and moved on.

replies.author: @AndyThompson1606
replies.text: ​@databangfunny how the great revolutionary war victors decided to stick with such an imperial relic 😂

replies.author: @databang
replies.text:
@johnx9318 (ಥ‿ಥ) well you were referring to United States in a condescending way and I thought I’d give a little nuance. Imperial units is a foundation of the Industrial Revolution and everyone used it as a standard, so you stating your countrys metric adoption is pretty much the same history as everyone else. However, as I stated in my last comment we’re not tyrannical about enforcement, nor get snooty about it because here in The USofA we are taught metric standards in science, medicine, as well as many sectors of industry and government, including the military. 

Conventionally for instance, there are anecdotal objections to the use of metric units in carpentry and the building trades, on the basis that it is easier to remember an integer number of inches plus a fraction than a measurement in millimeters, or that foot-inch measurements are more suitable when distances are frequently divided into halves, thirds, and quarters, often in parallel. The metric system also lacks a parallel measurement to the foot. 
👟🥿👠👞🥾👢👡🩰⛸️🛼
So, despite our lackadaisical metric adoption we’ve done okay getting a man to the moon and back using our primitive methods. Maybe one day your country may follow in our foot steps, but only if you count in shoes.

replies.author: @Alkimi
replies.text: Nope. Before that, ancient people needed to determine how many stones it would take to build a well, or a wall, or how much wheat could be grown in a particular field, how many apples would fit in a basket, or how much wood it would take to keep the fire burning all night, all winter, all year.

replies.author: @sharonjuniorchess
replies.text: @samsungtv4u the metric system uses water for its weights & measurement but  I believe weights and measures evolved locally. If I have a stone that is used as a unit of weight it is easy enough to check on a balance scale if another weight is equal. Then you simply scale up by doubling the weights. This system is still used today in some parts of the world.

replies.author: @samsungtv4u
replies.text: @sharonjuniorchess  I assumed at one time maybe there was a mathematical constant for a weight That may be used dimensions of an item relative to a waight , planck constant for example is dependent on joules which is dependent on Newton's and meters which are dependent on kilograms , so  it still can't really be used as a mathematical constant without having the kilogram available as a reference.     I assumed that Babylonians were really I'm good at astronomy And they would have a measurement of weight That is based on angles and solar system, But couldn't find anything interesting,  so far the most interesting thing has been what you mentioned use gravity and pendulum as a scale of measurement. Don't know why I never learned about it , I guess that's the US education system, just smart enough to pay taxes :)  . I Find it hard to believe that people lived without a standard measurement of weight there must be a simple standard that can mathematically be associated with all other units of measurement , 1 kg is equals to cm1000³ of water is a beautiful example,  for planet earth but outside of Earth This unit has no reference. Anyway thanks for your time I'm sure you have other things to do, love your videos I have learned a lot from you or at least you often direct me to research more about things so normally wouldn't think of. replies.author: @AnonymousAnarchist2
replies.text:
yes.
The answer is yes. Because it is both. Mathmatics are natural things, the ratio between perimiter and area is something you can observe without mathmatics, and it would still be mathmatics, just without maths to describe it.

However, the means that we decribe mathmatics was whole sale invented and has some issues we have to be mindful of. An famous exmaple being 1/3×3= .99999 forever, yet 3/3=1, the probem only shows up because we use base 10 other bases have other problematic numerals but not all of them have issues with thirds. 
So we know that part is fully invented, the nunbers thrmselves anf how we group them; and we can prove to ourselves that part is invented by taking three rocks, seperating them, then recombining them, and we can see there is no 4th rock created that shrinks away forever until its infinitaly small.
